summary: DHH responds to a bank developer's endorsement of Rails, addressing concerns about enterprise adoption. Michael Koziarski, who works at a bank with Java/COBOL infrastructure, praises Rails as the most productive web framework he's used but doubts its suitability for enterprise environments. DHH pushes back on two specific objections: the difficulty of finding Ruby programmers and the constraints imposed by DBAs. He argues that good programmers adapt quickly to new languages, and that DBA processes often add unnecessary complexity rather than value.
key_points: A Java enterprise developer at a bank endorses Rails as the most productive web framework while questioning its enterprise readiness
DHH argues hiring should focus on adaptable programmers rather than years of experience in specific technologies, citing a team of PHP developers who picked up Rails in a week
DBAs are characterized as 'distortion devices' who mangle simple schemas with nonsensical prefixes and speculative columns
DHH suggests routing around organizational bottlenecks like rigid DBA processes rather than abandoning productive tools
money_quotes: I've had some configuration files that are bigger than Basecamp (4kLoc)
Stop worrying too much about finding people with x years of experience in y and look for people who are adaptable and good programmers.
Good programmers can adapt to new environments much easier than most people expect.
It's likely going to take them longer getting up to speed on the specifics of your application than adapting to the environment.
Every time I discuss their role with other programmers I get the distinct impression that their primary role is as a distortion device.
So maybe you need to route around the damage if distortions is truly what you're getting back?
In the end, it's all about delivering the maximum amount of value to the stake holders.
key_insight: Enterprise objections to adopting productive tools like Rails often stem from organizational dysfunction—rigid hiring practices and bureaucratic gatekeepers—rather than genuine technical limitations.
